question: What is the current state of TB infection control among health workers, health care settings and congregate settings?
ground_truth: At present, the knowledge and practice on TB infection control among health workers, health care settings and congregate settings such as prisons is very low.
answer: According to the provided information, the current state of TB infection control among health workers, health care settings, and congregate settings in Uganda is low. Specifically, “the knowledge and practice on TB infection control among health workers, health care settings and congregate settings such as prisons is very low.” (Source: Ministry of Health Uganda, URL: https://library.health.go.ug/file-download/download/public/299). However, the Ministry of Health, in collaboration with partners like WHO, TB CAP, and RCQHC, has developed TB infection control guidelines and has built capacity in this area. Successful implementation requires urgent orientation to standard work practices (administrative control measures), environmental control measures, and protective measures, complemented by addressing challenges posed by older facility designs. (Source: Ministry of Health Uganda, URL: https://library.health.go.ug/file-download/download/public/299)

(Source: Ministry of Health Uganda, URL: https://health.go.ug/sites/default/files/NTLP%20Manual%203rd%20edition_17th%20Aug_final.pdf).

question: What is the term for Multi Drug Resistant TB?
ground_truth: MDR-TB
answer: Multidrug-resistant TB (MDR-TB) is defined as resistance to at least both isoniazid and rifampicin (Source: World Health Organisation, URL: https://www.who.int/teams/global-programme-on-tuberculosis-and-lung-health/tb-reports/global-tuberculosis-report-2024).  More specifically, it is caused by TB germs that are resistant to at least two of the most important TB medicines: isoniazid and rifampin (Source: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention, URL: https://www.cdc.gov/tb/media/Question_Answers_About_TB_English.pdf).

question: What is the association of Tuberculosis (TB) with HIV?
ground_truth: Tuberculosis is associated with HIV
answer: According to the provided information, HIV is the strongest risk factor for developing tuberculosis (TB) disease in those with latent or new *Mycobacterium tuberculosis* infection. People living with HIV (PLHIV) are 20 to 37 times more likely to develop TB than those without HIV infection (Source: Ministry of Health Uganda, URL: https://health.go.ug/sites/default/files/NTLP%20Manual%203rd%20edition_17th%20Aug_final.pdf). Furthermore, TB is the leading cause of HIV-related hospitalization and mortality, accounting for approximately 27-34% of deaths among hospitalized HIV-positive adults and 11-49% of deaths among HIV-positive children (Source: WHO HIV/TB update, 2015). In Uganda, an estimated 45% of TB patients are also infected with HIV (Source: Ministry of Health Uganda, URL: https://library.health.go.ug/sites/default/files/resources/Manual%20for%20management%20and%20Control%20of%20TB%20and%20Leprosy%202017l.pdf).

question: What settings are considered high-risk for transmitting TB?
ground_truth: congregate settings, such as schools and prisons
answer:
According to the provided information, several settings are considered high-risk for transmitting TB. These include:

*   Isolation rooms for patients with TB or MDR-TB (Source: Ministry of Health Uganda, URL: https://library.health.go.ug/file-download/download/public/299)
*   Collection and sputum induction rooms (Source: Ministry of Health Uganda, URL: https://library.health.go.ug/file-download/download/public/299)
*   X-ray rooms (Source: Ministry of Health Uganda, URL: https://library.health.go.ug/file-download/download/public/299)
*   Bronchoscopy rooms (Source: Ministry of Health Uganda, URL: https://library.health.go.ug/file-download/download/public/299)
*   Mortuaries (Source: Ministry of Health Uganda, URL: https://library.health.go.ug/file-download/download/public/299)
*   Spirometry rooms (Source: Ministry of Health Uganda, URL: https://library.health.go.ug/file-download/download/public/299)
*   Theatres handling surgery on potentially infectious TB patients (Source: Ministry of Health Uganda, URL: https://library.health.go.ug/file-download/download/public/299)

Additionally, the document highlights high-risk areas within referral hospitals and mentions group settings like jails, hospices, skilled nursing facilities, shelters, and other healthcare facilities as potential exposure locations (Source: Ministry of Health Uganda, URL: https://library.health.go.ug/file-download/download/public/299). Laboratories handling TB specimens are also identified as high-risk areas requiring specific controls (Source: Ministry of Health Uganda, URL: https://library.health.go.ug/file-download/download/public/299).

question: What are the three broad categories constituting a set of TB infection control measures?
ground_truth: There are three broad categories constituting a set of TB infection control measures: Administrative, Environmental, and Personal protective equipment.
answer:
The three broad categories constituting a set of TB infection control measures are:

1.  Administrative (work practices) (Source: Ministry of Health Uganda, URL: https://library.health.go.ug/file-download/download/public/299)
2.  Environmental (Source: Ministry of Health Uganda, URL: https://library.health.go.ug/file-download/download/public/299)
3.  Personal protective equipment (Source: Ministry of Health Uganda, URL: https://library.health.go.ug/file-download/download/public/299)

question: What is the effect when environmental controls and personal protective equipment are used together?
ground_truth: they contribute to a significant reduction of transmission of TB
answer: When environmental controls and personal protective equipment are used together, they contribute to a significant reduction of TB transmission. This is because environmental controls reduce the concentration of infectious TB germs in the air, while personal protective equipment, such as particulate respirators, prevents individuals from inhaling those germs. (Source: Ministry of Health Uganda, URL: https://library.health.go.ug/file-download/download/public/299)

question: What is the purpose of implementing TB infection control measures?
ground_truth: to create an enabling environment for the implementation of TB infection control in health-care facilities, congregate settings and households
answer: The purpose of instituting TB Infection Control (TB IC) measures is to minimize TB transmission within populations or congregate settings. Specifically, administrative measures are considered the most important level, aiming to stop the release of droplet nuclei into the air, thereby reducing exposure to *Mycobacterium tuberculosis* for healthcare workers, patients, and visitors. This involves promptly identifying, separating, educating, and treating patients with presumptive TB while minimizing stigma (Ministry of Health Uganda, URL: https://health.go.ug/sites/default/files/NTLP%20Manual%203rd%20edition_17th%20Aug_final.pdf).  Managerial activities at the health facility level also form the basis for implementing these measures effectively (Ministry of Health Uganda, URL: https://library.health.go.ug/file-download/download/public/299).

question: Who is the responsibility of at different levels to coordinate TB infection control activities?
ground_truth: The coordination of TB infection control activities.
answer:
According to the provided information, the responsibility for coordinating TB infection control activities falls to several levels:

*   **Health Care Facility Management:** Managers (in-charges) are responsible for nominating a TB infection control focal person on the infection control committee (Ministry of Health Uganda, URL: https://library.health.go.ug/file-download/download/public/299).
*   **District Infection Control Committees:** These committees should be formed where they are not already present and must include a TB infection control focal person (Ministry of Health Uganda, URL: https://library.health.go.ug/file-download/download/public/299).
*   **National TBIC Sub-committee:** Policy makers responsible for congregate settings should be part of this sub-committee for planning and implementation (Ministry of Health Uganda, URL: https://library.health.go.ug/file-download/download/public/299).

Additionally, national and district level managers are tasked with ensuring that TB infection control issues are included in national and district infection control plans and TB and HIV strategic plans (Ministry of Health Uganda, URL: https://library.health.go.ug/file-download/download/public/299).

question: What is necessary to enable healthcare workers to implement TB infection control measures?
ground_truth: The necessary support systems (such as training, supervision, supplying equipment, and disinfectants)
answer: To enable healthcare workers to implement TB infection control measures, they urgently need to be oriented to standard work practices (administrative control measures) and environmental and protective measures (Ministry of Health Uganda, URL: https://library.health.go.ug/file-download/download/public/299). Specifically, healthcare facility managers should ensure that workers are assessed for TB signs and symptoms, encouraged to know their HIV status, and that HIV-positive individuals are offered a change of duties to minimize exposure to those with TB (Ministry of Health Uganda, URL: https://library.health.go.ug/file-download/download/public/299). Furthermore, HIV-positive individuals should be screened for isoniazid preventive therapy as part of basic HIV care and treatment (Ministry of Health Uganda, URL: https://library.health.go.ug/file-download/download/public/299).

question: How often should HIV infection and TB disease surveillance be conducted among health care workers?
ground_truth: After proper counseling on a five year basis or when indicated
answer: Surveillance of HIV infection and TB disease among health care workers should be conducted after proper counseling on a five-year basis or when indicated and on every new staff. (Ministry of Health Uganda, URL: https://library.health.go.ug/file-download/download/public/299) Additionally, health care facilities should institutionalize TB screening of the new health care workers, five year or more often for selected staffs for TB infection and disease. (Ministry of Health Uganda, URL: https://library.health.go.ug/file-download/download/public/299)

question: How are TB infection control measures typically implemented?
ground_truth: Approaches should be prioritized where possible. They should consider human rights and the dignity of the patient and health care workers, and should balance the interests of individuals and public health.
answer: TB infection control measures are typically implemented at the health facility level through a combination of managerial, administrative, and environmental measures. Managerial activities ensure administrative commitment and leadership, forming the basis for implementing other controls (Ministry of Health Uganda, URL: https://library.health.go.ug/file-download/download/public/299). Administrative measures focus on preventing the release of droplet nuclei, promptly identifying and separating presumptive TB patients, educating them, and initiating effective treatment while minimizing stigma (Ministry of Health Uganda, URL: https://health.go.ug/sites/default/files/NTLP%20Manual%203rd%20edition_17th%20Aug_final.pdf). Environmental measures, such as opening windows and using mechanical ventilation, reduce the concentration of droplet nuclei in the air (Ministry of Health Uganda, URL: https://health.go.ug/sites/default/files/NTLP%20Manual%203rd%20edition_17th%20Aug_final.pdf). Additionally, on-site monitoring and evaluation, including facility tours and reviewing medical records, are carried out regularly to assess the implementation of TBIC and surveillance of TB disease among health care workers (Ministry of Health Uganda, URL: https://library.health.go.ug/file-download/download/public/299).

question: What is the recommended method for implementing TB infection control?
ground_truth: Use Behaviour-change campaigns
answer:
The recommended method for implementing TB infection control involves a combination of approaches, prioritizing administrative controls as the first step. Specifically, it includes:

1.  **Promptly identifying** individuals with TB symptoms and patients using the ICF form (Annex 1) (Ministry of Health Uganda, URL: https://library.health.go.ug/file-download/download/public/299).
2.  **Separating** presumptive TB patients into an appropriate environment (Ministry of Health Uganda, URL: https://health.go.ug/sites/default/files/NTLP%20Manual%203rd%20edition_17th%20Aug_final.pdf).
3.  **Tracing and screening** close contacts and treating them if infected (Ministry of Health Uganda, URL: https://health.go.ug/sites/default/files/NTLP%20Manual%203rd%20edition_17th%20Aug_final.pdf).
4.  **Minimizing unnecessary hospitalizations and outpatient visits**, reducing overcrowding, and utilizing community-care approaches (Ministry of Health Uganda, URL: https://library.health.go.ug/file-download/download/public/299).

Furthermore, the implementation should be overseen by a designated TB infection control focal person within the facility’s infection control committee (Ministry of Health Uganda, URL: https://library.health.go.ug/file-download/download/public/299). Regular on-site surveillance and assessments are also crucial to evaluate risk and identify needed improvements (Ministry of Health Uganda, URL: https://library.health.go.ug/file-download/download/public/299).

ground_truth: Ensuring that close contacts and families of patients with TB are traced, screened and treated if infected.

question: What is one way to ensure that patients with TB are adhering to treatment?
ground_truth: Ensuring adequate ventilation and natural lighting
answer: One way to ensure that patients with TB are adhering to treatment is through Directly Observed Therapy (DOT). This involves arranging and initiating DOT by engaging a suitable treatment supporter, such as a family member, trusted individual, or community health worker (Ministry of Health Uganda, URL: https://library.health.go.ug/sites/default/files/resources/TB%20and%20Leprosy%20Case%20Management%20Desk%20Guide_3rd%20Edition_22_2_2019.pdf). A healthcare worker will regularly observe the patient taking their medication, ensuring they take the correct dosage at the correct intervals (Ministry of Health Knowledge Management Portal, URL: https://library.health.go.ug/communicable-disease/tuberculosis/treatment-tuberculosis-guidelines-fourth-edition).

question: Can tuberculosis be spread by coughing and/or sneezing?
ground_truth: Yes, TB can be spread by coughing and/or sneezing
answer: Yes, tuberculosis can be spread by coughing and/or sneezing. According to the Mayo Clinic, “Tuberculosis can spread when a person with the illness coughs, sneezes or sings. This can put tiny droplets with the germs into the air.” (Source: Mayo Clinic, URL: https://www.mayoclinic.org/diseases-conditions/tuberculosis/symptoms-causes/syc-20351250). The Centers for Disease Control and Prevention also states that “Active TB disease in the lungs or throat can be infectious.” (Source: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention, URL: https://www.cdc.gov/tb/causes/index.html).

question: What essential items are required to be available for TB infection control?
ground_truth: respirators, sputum mugs and disinfectants
answer:
The provided information outlines several essential items for TB infection control. These include:

*   **N95 masks:** These are worn by healthcare workers to protect them from inhaling TB germs (Source: Ministry of Health Uganda, URL: https://library.health.go.ug/sites/default/files/resources/TB%20and%20Leprosy%20Case%20Management%20Desk%20Guide_3rd%20Edition_22_2_2019.pdf).
*   **Surgical masks:** These are worn by patients (Source: Ministry of Health Uganda, URL: https://library.health.go.ug/sites/default/files/resources/TB%20and%20Leprosy%20Case%20Management%20Desk%20Guide_3rd%20Edition_22_2_2019.pdf).
*   **Natural ventilation and mechanical ventilation (fans):** These are environmental measures to help control the release of TB germs into the air (Source: Ministry of Health Uganda, URL: https://library.health.go.ug/sites/default/files/resources/TB%20and%20Leprosy%20Case%20Management%20Desk%20Guide_3rd%20Edition_22_2_2019.pdf).
*   **Tissues and face masks:** Providing these to patients for cough etiquette and respiratory hygiene (Source: Ministry of Health Uganda, URL: https://library.health.go.ug/file-download/download/public/299).

question: What are the symptoms that health care workers should know to seek a health assessment including screening for TB?
ground_truth: The symptoms of TB
answer:
The provided information outlines several symptoms that healthcare workers should be aware of to assess for potential TB infection. These include:

*   A fever (Source: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention, URL: https://www.cdc.gov/tb/media/Staying_on_Track_with_TB_Medicine.pdf)
*   A rash (Source: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention, URL: https://www.cdc.gov/tb/media/Staying_on_Track_with_TB_Medicine.pdf)
*   Aching joints (Source: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention, URL: https://www.cdc.gov/tb/media/Staying_on_Track_with_TB_Medicine.pdf)
*   Aches or tingling in fingers or toes (Source: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention, URL: https://www.cdc.gov/tb/media/Staying_on_Track_with_TB_Medicine.pdf)
*   An upset stomach, nausea, or stomach cramps (Source: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention, URL: https://www.cdc.gov/tb/media/Staying_on_Track_with_TB_Medicine.pdf)
*   Vomiting (Source: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention, URL: https://www.cdc.gov/tb/media/Staying_on_Track_with_TB_Medicine.pdf)
*   Changes in eyesight such as blurred vision (Source: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention, URL: https://www.cdc.gov/tb/media/Staying_on_Track_with_TB_Medicine.pdf)
*   Changes in hearing such as ringing in the ears (Source: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention, URL: https://www.cdc.gov/tb/media/Staying_on_Track_with_TB_Medicine.pdf)
*   Dizziness (Source: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention, URL: https://www.cdc.gov/tb/media/Staying_on_Track_with_TB_Medicine.pdf)
*   Bruising (Source: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention, URL: https://www.cdc.gov/tb/media/Staying_on_Track_with_TB_Medicine.pdf)
*   Easy bleeding with cuts (Source: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention, URL: https://www.cdc.gov/tb/media/Staying_on_Track_with_TB_Medicine.pdf)
*   Less appetite or no appetite (Source: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention, URL: https://www.cdc.gov/tb/media/Staying_on_Track_with_TB_Medicine.pdf)
*   Tingling or numbness around the mouth (Source: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention, URL: https://www.cdc.gov/tb/media/Staying_on_Track_with_TB_Medicine.pdf)
*   Yellow skin or eyes (Source: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention, URL: https://www.cdc.gov/tb/media/Staying_on_Track_with_TB_Medicine.pdf)

Additionally, the following symptoms are indicative of TB and should prompt further investigation:

*   Prolonged cough (sometimes with blood) (Source: Ministry of Health Uganda, URL: https://library.health.go.ug/file-download/download/public/299)
*   Chest pain (Source: Mayo Clinic, URL: https://www.mayoclinic.org/diseases-conditions/tuberculosis/symptoms-causes/syc-20351250)
*   Weakness (Source: Mayo Clinic, URL: https://www.mayoclinic.org/diseases-conditions/tuberculosis/symptoms-causes/syc-20351250)
*   Fatigue (Source: Mayo Clinic, URL: https://www.mayoclinic.org/diseases-conditions/tuberculosis/symptoms-causes/syc-20351250)
*   Weight loss (Source: Mayo Clinic, URL: https://www.mayoclinic.org/diseases-conditions/tuberculosis/symptoms-causes/syc-20351250)
*   Night sweats (Source: Mayo Clinic, URL: https://www.mayoclinic.org/diseases-conditions/tuberculosis/symptoms-causes/syc-20351250)

question: What is a vital part of sound infection control practices for individuals suspected of having TB infection?
ground_truth: laughs or spits
answer: A vital part of sound infection control practices for individuals suspected of having TB infection is promptly identifying, separating, and treating them, as well as tracing and screening their close contacts (Source: Ministry of Health Uganda, URL: https://library.health.go.ug/file-download/download/public/299). Additionally, educating patients on cough etiquette and respiratory hygiene, such as covering their mouths and noses when coughing or sneezing, is crucial (Source: Ministry of Health Uganda, URL: https://library.health.go.ug/file-download/download/public/299).

question: What is cough etiquette in relation to tuberculosis?
ground_truth: reduces the release of TB germs into the air
answer: Cough etiquette, in relation to tuberculosis, involves educating patients – and healthcare workers, visitors, and families – on proper respiratory hygiene. This includes covering their mouths and noses when coughing or sneezing to reduce the release of TB germs into the air and contribute to controlling TB and other respiratory infections. (Source: Ministry of Health Uganda, URL: https://library.health.go.ug/file-download/download/public/299)

question: What type of environment is recommended for collecting sputum samples for TB diagnosis?

answer: According to the Ministry of Health Uganda, sputum should be collected in a designated area with a lot of air circulation, away from other people [https://library.health.go.ug/file-download/download/public/299]. question: How long does it typically take to confirm the diagnosis of tuberculosis (TB) disease?
ground_truth: 2-6 weeks
answer: According to the Ministry of Health Uganda, the time to confirm the diagnosis of TB disease should not exceed 24 hours for sputum smear microscopy results and 2-6 weeks for culture. [https://library.health.go.ug/file-download/download/public/299]

question: What should be done to control the release of TB germs into the air?
ground_truth: through educating on cough etiquette and respiratory hygiene
answer: To control the release of TB germs into the air, the Ministry of Health Uganda recommends several measures [https://library.health.go.ug/file-download/download/public/299]. These include opening windows to allow for air circulation and using fans to exhaust air from the room [https://library.health.go.ug/file-download/download/public/299]. Additionally, using particulate respirators is recommended in situations where there is an increased risk of transmission [https://library.health.go.ug/file-download/download/public/299]. Environmental control measures, such as opening windows and using mechanical ventilation, complement administrative measures to reduce the concentration of TB germs in the air [https://library.health.go.ug/file-download/download/public/299].

question: What is the recommended action for close contacts of a patient with tuberculosis?
ground_truth: Health visitors or staff assigned to TB infection control committee should bring in the patient’s close contacts and family members for screening and treatment if found TB infected
answer: The Ministry of Health Uganda recommends systematic screening of all household contacts and close contacts of TB patients for active TB. Contact investigation is key to interrupting spread, identifying people with TB disease and HIV, and initiating them on treatment early. Close contacts are defined as those who have spent one or more nights, or frequent or extended periods during the day, with a TB index case within the 3 months prior to the diagnosis and TB treatment start. [https://library.health.go.ug/sites/default/files/resources/TB%20and%20Leprosy%20Case%20Management%20Desk%20Guide_3rd%20Edition_22_2_2019.pdf] Furthermore, health visitors or staff assigned to TB infection control committees should bring in the patient’s close contacts and family members for screening and treatment if found TB infected. [https://library.health.go.ug/file-download/download/public/299]

question: What type of personnel should receive TB infection control training?
ground_truth: health care workers including staffs, cleaners, drivers, gate keepers, peer educators, adherence supporters, and volunteers working as counselors
answer: According to the Ministry of Health Uganda, all health care workers, including staff, cleaners, drivers, gatekeepers, peer educators, adherence supporters, and volunteers who counsel TB patients, need to receive TB infection control training [https://library.health.go.ug/file-download/download/public/299]. Furthermore, health care facilities should conduct TB screening of new workers and selected staff every five years or more [https://library.health.go.ug/file-download/download/public/299].

question: What type of treatment is offered to health care workers diagnosed with active TB?
ground_truth: a full regimen of anti-TB treatment
answer: According to the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention, if a health care worker is diagnosed with active TB disease, they can be treated with medicine and will need to take and finish all of their TB medicine as directed by their doctor or nurse [https://www.cdc.gov/tb/media/pdfs/What_You_Need_to_Know_About_TB.pdf]. The best way to ensure adherence is through directly observed therapy (DOT), where a health care worker meets with the worker daily or several times a week to monitor medication intake [https://www.cdc.gov/tb/media/pdfs/What_You_Need_to_Know_About_TB.pdf]. Furthermore, health care workers diagnosed with active TB should be put on either isoniazid preventive therapy (IPT) or a full regimen of anti-TB treatment [https://library.health.go.ug/file-download/download/public/299].

question: What should be done with healthcare workers if they have untreated TB?
ground_truth: They should be relocated from positions where exposure to untreated TB is high, to a lower risk area.
answer: If a healthcare worker is diagnosed with active TB, they should be relocated from positions where exposure to untreated TB is high, to a lower-risk area [https://library.health.go.ug/file-download/download/public/299]. Additionally, HIV-positive healthcare workers should not be working with patients, particularly those with MDR-TB and XDR-TB [https://library.health.go.ug/file-download/download/public/299].

question: What are environmental control measures for tuberculosis?
ground_truth: Environmental control measures decrease the concentration of the TB germs in the room.
answer:
Environmental control measures for tuberculosis decrease the concentration of TB germs in the room by controlling air movement and reducing the concentration of infectious TB germs in the air. These measures can be used in high-risk areas to prevent health care workers, patients, attendants, and visitors from inhaling air contaminated with TB germs [https://library.health.go.ug/file-download/download/public/299]. 

The Ministry of Health Uganda recommends using natural ventilation, mechanical ventilation systems (like fans), and supplemented with ultraviolet germicidal irradiation (UVGI) where adequate ventilation is difficult to achieve [https://library.health.go.ug/file-download/download/public/299].  These measures are most effective when combined with administrative and personal protective equipment [https://library.health.go.ug/file-download/download/public/299].

question: How does natural ventilation affect tuberculosis germs?
ground_truth: Natural ventilation removes and dilutes TB germ containing air in the room
answer: Natural ventilation removes and dilutes TB germ-containing air in the room. It relies on open doors and windows, along with fans, to bring in fresh air and remove stale air, thereby reducing the concentration of TB germs [https://library.health.go.ug/file-download/download/public/299].

question: What is recommended for improving air circulation in a building?
ground_truth: Windows that are opposite to each other should be opened.
answer: To improve air circulation, it is recommended to open windows and doors that are opposite each other to improve air dilution. Additionally, using fans, particularly ceiling fans, in conjunction with open windows enhances this process [https://library.health.go.ug/sites/default/files/NTLP%20Manual%203rd%20edition_17th%20Aug_final.pdf]. Natural ventilation, achieving at least 8-12 air changes per hour (ACH), is considered adequate and cost-effective in many health facilities [https://library.health.go.ug/sites/default/files/NTLP%20Manual%203rd%20edition_17th%20Aug_final.pdf].

question: Can mechanical ventilation be used to reduce the concentration of TB germs?
ground_truth: yes
answer: Yes, mechanical ventilation can be used to reduce the concentration of TB germs. According to the Ministry of Health Uganda, “Mechanical ventilation can be used to reduce the concentration of TB germs in the room where natural ventilation is not feasible or is inadequate.” [https://library.health.go.ug/file-download/download/public/299] It can be achieved through window fans and exhaust ventilation systems, particularly in isolation rooms and laboratories. [https://library.health.go.ug/file-download/download/public/299]
